perturbation.original_text: The CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION is disclosed in confidence so that HUCOM may evaluate and use CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION for the purpose of assisting VASCO in the commercial exploitation thereof. In consideration of the disclosure, HUCOM agrees to treat, and will treat, the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION disclosed to it as confidential until such time as the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION becomes publicly available through no act or failure to act on the part of HUCOM as evidenced by written documentation.
perturbation.changed_text: The CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION is disclosed in confidence so that HUCOM may evaluate and use CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION for the purpose of assisting VASCO in the commercial exploitation thereof. In consideration of the disclosure, HUCOM agrees to treat, and will treat, the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION disclosed to it as confidential until such time as the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION becomes publicly available, regardless of the cause.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the confidentiality clause to state that information becomes non-confidential regardless of the cause undermines the purpose of a confidentiality agreement, which is to protect information from being disclosed due to actions or failures of the receiving party.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret in a way that requires efforts to maintain its secrecy. This modification negates the responsibility to maintain secrecy, as the information can become public regardless of efforts to protect it, directly contradicting the act's definition and protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified text eliminates the requirement that the receiving party (HUCOM) must fail to act to cause the confidential information to become public. The original text states the information is protected 'through no act or failure to act on the part of HUCOM'. The changed text removes this condition, stating the information becomes public regardless of the cause. As the Law Explanation notes, this contradicts the UTSA's definition of a trade secret, which requires efforts to maintain secrecy. The modification removes any such responsibility, creating a legal contradiction. The scraped snippets do not contain explicit law snippets; however, the UTSA definition explanation supports a finding of contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: HUCOM further agrees not to make any use of the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION other than for the above-mentioned purpose(s) and will not disclose CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to any other person without the prior written consent of VASCO, except that if HUCOM is a corporation, CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION may be disclosed to a person within the company on a need-to-know basis.
perturbation.changed_text: HUCOM further agrees not to make any use of the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION other than for the above-mentioned purpose(s) and will not disclose CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION to any other person without the prior written consent of VASCO, except that if HUCOM is a corporation, CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION may be disclosed to any person within the company.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'need-to-know basis' clause for internal disclosures within HUCOM broadens the scope of permissible disclosures, potentially jeopardizing the confidentiality of the information and increasing the risk of unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act criminalizes the misappropriation of trade secrets with the intent to benefit someone other than the owner. By removing the 'need-to-know' restriction, the contract increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation within HUCOM, potentially leading to violations of the Act if such misappropriation occurs with the requisite intent.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 90 / § 1832 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 1832 : Theft of trade secrets Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 90-PROTECTION OF TRADE SECRETS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments §1832. Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'need-to-know' restriction, potentially increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation, which the Economic Espionage Act (EEA) criminalizes when done with the intent to benefit someone other than the owner and injure the owner, as per 18 U.S.C. 1832. The original contract limited disclosure within the company to a need-to-know basis, safeguarding confidential information. Removing this restriction contradicts the intent of the EEA, creating a higher risk of illegal activity by broadening access to trade secrets within HUCOM without the safeguard.

perturbation.original_text: If no satisfactory arrangement is concluded between the parties, or if otherwise requested by VASCO, HUCOM agrees to return to VASCO any written disclosure of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION provided by VASCO plus any copies, notes, summaries or other materials derived from the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION.
perturbation.changed_text: If no arrangement is concluded between the parties, HUCOM agrees to return to VASCO any written disclosure of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, unless HUCOM deems them necessary for future development, in which case they may be retained indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: This allows HUCOM to retain confidential information indefinitely, which contradicts the purpose of the confidentiality agreement and the implied obligation to protect and return the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA allows trade secret owners to bring civil actions for misappropriation. This change allows HUCOM to retain confidential information under vague conditions, increasing the risk of misappropriation and thus creating a scenario where HUCOM could be in violation of the DTSA.

perturbation.justification: The change in the contract allows HUCOM to retain confidential information indefinitely if they deem it necessary for future development. This directly contradicts the purpose of a confidentiality agreement, which is to protect and return confidential information. The DTSA, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, allows trade secret owners to bring civil actions for misappropriation. By allowing HUCOM to retain confidential information under vague conditions, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation, creating a scenario where HUCOM could be in violation of the DTSA. Snippet 1 mentions the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, which provides a basis for legal action related to trade secret misappropriation. The modified contract weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with the DTSA's provisions against misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: With respect to the subject matter set forth above, this Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes any previous oral or written representations, understandings or agreements as to the above subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to the subject matter set forth above, this Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties, except for any implied understandings or representations made prior to this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that implied understandings are not superseded, the clause contradicts the intent of an integration clause, which is to ensure that the written contract is the complete and final agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Parol Evidence Rule generally prevents the introduction of prior or contemporaneous agreements to contradict a fully integrated written contract. This modification opens the door for such evidence, undermining the integrity of the written agreement and potentially leading to disputes over the actual terms of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the integration clause by allowing 'implied understandings or representations made prior to this agreement' to be considered part of the agreement, thus contradicting the original intent to supersede all prior agreements. This directly undermines the Parol Evidence Rule, as stated in the Law Explanation, which aims to prevent the introduction of prior agreements that contradict the written contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that terms in a final written agreement 'may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement'. The modification opens the door for disputes and undermines the integrity of the written agreement.
